question:
Start with a finite sequence $a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n$ of positive integers.
If possible, choose two indices $j < k$ such that $a_j$ does not divide
$a_k$, and replace $a_j$ and $a_k$ by $\mathrm{gcd}(a_j, a_k)$
and $\mathrm{lcm}(a_j, a_k)$, respectively. Prove that if this process is
repeated, it must eventually stop and the final sequence does not depend
on the choices made. (Note: gcd means greatest common divisor and lcm
means least common multiple.)
solution:
We first prove that the process stops. Note first that the product
$a_1 \cdots a_n$ remains constant, because
$a_j a_k = \gcd(a_j, a_k) \lcm(a_j, a_k)$. Moreover,
the last number in the sequence can never decrease, because it is always
replaced by its least common multiple with another number.
Since it is bounded above (by the product
of all of the numbers), the last number must eventually reach its
maximum value, after which it remains constant throughout. After this
happens, the next-to-last number will never decrease, so it eventually
becomes constant, and so on. After finitely many steps, all of the numbers
will achieve their final values, so
no more steps will be possible. This only happens when
$a_j$ divides $a_k$ for all pairs $j < k$.

We next check that there is only one possible final sequence.
For $p$ a prime and $m$ a nonnegative integer, we claim that the number
of integers in the list divisible by $p^m$ never changes. To see this,
suppose we replace $a_j, a_k$ by $\gcd(a_j, a_k),\lcm(a_j,a_k)$.
If neither of $a_j, a_k$ is divisible by $p^m$, then neither of
$\gcd(a_j, a_k),\lcm(a_j,a_k)$ is either.
If exactly one $a_j, a_k$ is divisible by $p^m$, then
$\lcm(a_j,a_k)$ is divisible by $p^m$ but $\gcd(a_j, a_k)$ is not.

$\gcd(a_j, a_k),\lcm(a_j,a_k)$ are as well.

If we started out with exactly $h$ numbers not divisible by $p^m$,
then in the final sequence $a'_1, \dots, a'_n$, the numbers
$a'_{h+1}, \dots, a'_n$ are divisible by $p^m$ while the numbers
$a'_1, \dots, a'_h$ are not. Repeating this argument for each
pair $(p,m)$ such that $p^m$ divides the initial product
$a_1,\dots,a_n$, we can determine the exact prime factorization
of each of $a'_1,\dots,a'_n$. This proves that the final sequence
is unique.

\textbf{Remark:}
(by David Savitt and Noam Elkies)
Here are two other ways to prove the termination.
One is to observe that $\prod_j a_j^j$
is \emph{strictly} increasing at each step, and bounded above by
$(a_1\cdots a_n)^n$. The other is to notice that $a_1$ is nonincreasing
but always positive, so eventually becomes constant; then
$a_2$ is nonincreasing but always positive, and so on.

\textbf{Reinterpretation:}
For each $p$, consider the sequence consisting of the
exponents of $p$ in the prime factorizations of $a_1,\dots,a_n$.
At each step, we pick two positions $i$ and $j$ such that the exponents
of some prime $p$ are in the wrong order at positions $i$ and $j$.
We then sort these two position into the correct order for every prime $p$
simultaneously.

It is clear that this can only terminate with all sequences being sorted
into the correct order. We must still check that the process terminates;
however, since all but finitely many of the exponent
sequences consist of all zeroes,
and each step makes a nontrivial switch in at least one of the other exponent
sequences, it is enough to check the case of a single exponent sequence.
This can be done as in the first solution.

\textbf{Remark:}
Abhinav Kumar suggests the following proof that the process always terminates
in at most  $\binom{n}{2}$ steps.
(This is a variant of the worst-case analysis of the \emph{bubble sort}
algorithm.)

Consider the number of pairs $(k,l)$
with $1 \leq k < l \leq n$ such that $a_k$ does not divide $a_l$
(call these \emph{bad pairs}).
At each step, we find one bad pair $(i,j)$ and eliminate it, and we do not
touch any pairs that do not involve either $i$ or $j$.
If $i < k < j$, then neither of the pairs $(i,k)$ and $(k,j)$ can become
bad,
because $a_i$ is replaced by a divisor of itself, while $a_j$ is replaced by
a multiple of itself. If $k < i$, then $(k,i)$ can only become a bad pair if
$a_k$ divided $a_i$ but not $a_j$, in which case $(k,j)$ stops being bad.
Similarly, if $k > j$, then $(i,k)$ and $(j,k)$
either stay the same or switch status. Hence the number of bad pairs
goes down by at least 1 each time; since it is at most $\binom{n}{2}$
to begin with, this is an upper bound for the number of steps.

\textbf{Remark:}
This problem is closely related to the classification theorem for
finite abelian groups. Namely, if $a_1,\dots,a_n$
and $a'_1,\dots,a'_n$ are the sequences obtained at two different
steps in the process, then the abelian groups
$\ZZ/a_1 \ZZ \times \cdots \times \ZZ/a_n \ZZ$
and
$\ZZ/a'_1 \ZZ \times \cdots \times \ZZ/a'_n \ZZ$
are isomorphic. The final sequence gives a canonical
presentation of this group; the terms of this sequence are called the
\emph{elementary divisors} or \emph{invariant factors} of the group.

\textbf{Remark:} (by Tom Belulovich)
A \emph{lattice} is a partially ordered set $L$ in which for
any two $x,y \in L$, there is a unique minimal element $z$ with $z \geq
x$ and $z \geq y$, called the \emph{join} and denoted $x \wedge y$,
and there is a unique maximal element $z$ with $z \leq x$ and $z \leq y$,
called the \emph{meet} and denoted $x \vee y$. In terms of a lattice $L$,
one can pose the following generalization of the given problem.
Start with $a_1,\dots,a_n \in L$. If $i < j$ but $a_i \not\leq a_j$,
it is permitted to replace $a_i, a_j$ by $a_i \vee a_j, a_i \wedge a_j$,
respectively. The same argument as above shows that this always terminates
in at most $\binom{n}{2}$ steps. The question is, under what conditions on
the lattice $L$ is the final sequence uniquely determined by the initial
sequence?

It turns out that this holds if and only if $L$ is \emph{distributive},
i.e., for any $x,y,z \in L$,
\[
x \wedge (y \vee z)
= (x \wedge y) \vee (x \wedge z).
\]
(This is equivalent to the same axiom with the operations interchanged.)
For example, if $L$ is a \emph{Boolean algebra}, i.e., the set of subsets
of a given set $S$ under inclusion, then $\wedge$ is union, $\vee$
is intersection, and the distributive law holds.
Conversely, any finite
distributive lattice is contained in a Boolean algebra by a theorem of
Birkhoff. The correspondence takes each  $x \in L$ to the set of
$y \in L$ such that $x \geq y$ and $y$ cannot be written as a join
of two elements of $L \setminus \{y\}$. (See for instance
Birkhoff, \textit{Lattice Theory}, Amer. Math. Soc., 1967.)

On one hand, if $L$ is distributive, it can be shown that the $j$-th term
of the final sequence is equal to the meet of $a_{i_1} \wedge \cdots
\wedge a_{i_j}$ over all sequences $1 \leq i_1 < \cdots < i_j \leq n$.
For instance, this can be checked by forming the smallest subset $L'$
of $L$ containing $a_1,\dots,a_n$ and closed under meet and join,
then embedding $L'$ into a Boolean algebra using
Birkhoff's theorem, then checking the claim for all Boolean algebras.
It can also be checked directly (as suggested by Nghi Nguyen)
by showing that for $j=1,\dots,n$,
the meet of all joins of $j$-element subsets of $a_1,\dots,a_n$ is
invariant at each step.

On the other hand,
a lattice fails to be distributive if and only if
it contains five elements $a,b,c,0,1$ such that either the only relations
among them are implied by
\[
1 \geq a,b,c \geq 0
\]
(this lattice is sometimes called the \emph{diamond}),
or the only relations among them  are implied by
\[
1 \geq a \geq b \geq 0, \qquad 1 \geq c \geq 0
\]
(this lattice is sometimes called the \emph{pentagon}).
(For a proof, see the Birkhoff reference given above.) For each of these
examples, the initial sequence $a,b,c$ fails to determine the final
sequence; for the diamond, we can end up with $0, *, 1$ for
any of $* = a,b,c$, whereas for the pentagon we can end up with
$0, *, 1$ for any of $* = a, b$.

Consequently, the final sequence is determined by the initial sequence
if and only if $L$ is distributive.

descriptive_long.question:
Start with a finite sequence $elementone, elementtwo, \dots, elementn$ of positive integers.
If possible, choose two indices $indexj < indexk$ such that $elementj$ does not divide
$elementk$, and replace $elementj$ and $elementk$ by $\mathrm{gcd}(elementj, elementk)$
and $\mathrm{lcm}(elementj, elementk)$, respectively. Prove that if this process is
repeated, it must eventually stop and the final sequence does not depend
on the choices made. (Note: gcd means greatest common divisor and lcm
means least common multiple.)
descriptive_long.solution:
We first prove that the process stops. Note first that the product
$elementone \cdots elementn$ remains constant, because
$elementj\,elementk = \gcd(elementj, elementk) \lcm(elementj, elementk)$. Moreover,
the last number in the sequence can never decrease, because it is always
replaced by its least common multiple with another number.
Since it is bounded above (by the product
of all of the numbers), the last number must eventually reach its
maximum value, after which it remains constant throughout. After this
happens, the next-to-last number will never decrease, so it eventually
becomes constant, and so on. After finitely many steps, all of the numbers
will achieve their final values, so
no more steps will be possible. This only happens when
$elementj$ divides $elementk$ for all pairs $indexj < indexk$.

We next check that there is only one possible final sequence.
For $primep$ a prime and $indexm$ a nonnegative integer, we claim that the number
of integers in the list divisible by $primep^{indexm}$ never changes. To see this,
suppose we replace $elementj, elementk$ by $\gcd(elementj, elementk),\lcm(elementj,elementk)$.
If neither of $elementj, elementk$ is divisible by $primep^{indexm}$, then neither of
$\gcd(elementj, elementk),\lcm(elementj,elementk)$ is either.
If exactly one $elementj, elementk$ is divisible by $primep^{indexm}$, then
$\lcm(elementj,elementk)$ is divisible by $primep^{indexm}$ but $\gcd(elementj, elementk)$ is not.

$\gcd(elementj, elementk),\lcm(elementj,elementk)$ are as well.

If we started out with exactly $counth$ numbers not divisible by $primep^{indexm}$,
then in the final sequence $baseseq'_1, \dots, baseseq'_{indexn}$, the numbers
$baseseq'_{counth+1}, \dots, baseseq'_{indexn}$ are divisible by $primep^{indexm}$ while the numbers
$baseseq'_1, \dots, baseseq'_{counth}$ are not. Repeating this argument for each
pair $(primep,indexm)$ such that $primep^{indexm}$ divides the initial product
$elementone,\dots,elementn$, we can determine the exact prime factorization
of each of $baseseq'_1,\dots,baseseq'_{indexn}$. This proves that the final sequence
is unique.

\textbf{Remark:}
(by David Savitt and Noam Elkies)
Here are two other ways to prove the termination.
One is to observe that $\prod_{indexj} elementj^{indexj}$
is \emph{strictly} increasing at each step, and bounded above by
$(elementone\cdots elementn)^{indexn}$. The other is to notice that $elementone$ is nonincreasing
but always positive, so eventually becomes constant; then
$elementtwo$ is nonincreasing but always positive, and so on.

\textbf{Reinterpretation:}
For each $primep$, consider the sequence consisting of the
exponents of $primep$ in the prime factorizations of $elementone,\dots,elementn$.
At each step, we pick two positions $indexi$ and $indexj$ such that the exponents
of some prime $primep$ are in the wrong order at positions $indexi$ and $indexj$.
We then sort these two positions into the correct order for every prime $primep$
simultaneously.

It is clear that this can only terminate with all sequences being sorted
into the correct order. We must still check that the process terminates;
however, since all but finitely many of the exponent
sequences consist of all zeroes,
and each step makes a nontrivial switch in at least one of the other exponent
sequences, it is enough to check the case of a single exponent sequence.
This can be done as in the first solution.

\textbf{Remark:}
Abhinav Kumar suggests the following proof that the process always terminates
in at most  $\binom{indexn}{2}$ steps.
(This is a variant of the worst-case analysis of the \emph{bubble sort}
algorithm.)

Consider the number of pairs $(indexk,indexl)$
with $1 \leq indexk < indexl \leq indexn$ such that $elementk$ does not divide $baseseq_{indexl}$
(call these \emph{bad pairs}).
At each step, we find one bad pair $(indexi,indexj)$ and eliminate it, and we do not
touch any pairs that do not involve either $indexi$ or $indexj$.
If $indexi < indexk < indexj$, then neither of the pairs $(indexi,indexk)$ and $(indexk,indexj)$ can become
bad,
because $elementi$ is replaced by a divisor of itself, while $elementj$ is replaced by
a multiple of itself. If $indexk < indexi$, then $(indexk,indexi)$ can only become a bad pair if
$elementk$ divided $elementi$ but not $elementj$, in which case $(indexk,indexj)$ stops being bad.
Similarly, if $indexk > indexj$, then $(indexi,indexk)$ and $(indexj,indexk)$
either stay the same or switch status. Hence the number of bad pairs
goes down by at least 1 each time; since it is at most $\binom{indexn}{2}$
to begin with, this is an upper bound for the number of steps.

\textbf{Remark:}
This problem is closely related to the classification theorem for
finite abelian groups. Namely, if $elementone,\dots,elementn$
and $baseseq'_1,\dots,baseseq'_{indexn}$ are the sequences obtained at two different
steps in the process, then the abelian groups
$\ZZ/elementone \ZZ \times \cdots \times \ZZ/elementn \ZZ$
and
$\ZZ/baseseq'_1 \ZZ \times \cdots \times \ZZ/baseseq'_{indexn} \ZZ$
are isomorphic. The final sequence gives a canonical
presentation of this group; the terms of this sequence are called the
\emph{elementary divisors} or \emph{invariant factors} of the group.

\textbf{Remark:} (by Tom Belulovich)
A \emph{lattice} is a partially ordered set $latticecap$ in which for
any two $latticex,latticey \in latticecap$, there is a unique minimal element $latticez$ with $latticez \geq
latticex$ and $latticez \geq latticey$, called the \emph{join} and denoted $latticex \wedge latticey$,
and there is a unique maximal element $latticez$ with $latticez \leq latticex$ and $latticez \leq latticey$,
called the \emph{meet} and denoted $latticex \vee latticey$. In terms of a lattice $latticecap$,
one can pose the following generalization of the given problem.
Start with $elementone,\dots,elementn \in latticecap$. If $indexi < indexj$ but $elementi \not\leq elementj$,
it is permitted to replace $elementi, elementj$ by $elementi \vee elementj, elementi \wedge elementj$,
respectively. The same argument as above shows that this always terminates
in at most $\binom{indexn}{2}$ steps. The question is, under what conditions on
the lattice $latticecap$ is the final sequence uniquely determined by the initial
sequence?

It turns out that this holds if and only if $latticecap$ is \emph{distributive},
i.e., for any $latticex,latticey,latticez \in latticecap$,
\[
latticex \wedge (latticey \vee latticez)
= (latticex \wedge latticey) \vee (latticex \wedge latticez).
\]
(This is equivalent to the same axiom with the operations interchanged.)
For example, if $latticecap$ is a \emph{Boolean algebra}, i.e., the set of subsets
of a given set $setcapital$ under inclusion, then $\wedge$ is union, $\vee$
is intersection, and the distributive law holds.
Conversely, any finite
distributive lattice is contained in a Boolean algebra by a theorem of
Birkhoff. The correspondence takes each  $latticex \in latticecap$ to the set of
$latticey \in latticecap$ such that $latticex \geq latticey$ and $latticey$ cannot be written as a join
of two elements of $latticecap \setminus \{latticey\}$. (See for instance
Birkhoff, \textit{Lattice Theory}, Amer. Math. Soc., 1967.)

On one hand, if $latticecap$ is distributive, it can be shown that the $indexj$-th term
of the final sequence is equal to the meet of $baseseq_{i_1} \wedge \cdots
\wedge baseseq_{i_{indexj}}$ over all sequences $1 \leq i_1 < \cdots < i_{indexj} \leq indexn$.
For instance, this can be checked by forming the smallest subset $latticecap'$
of $latticecap$ containing $elementone,\dots,elementn$ and closed under meet and join,
then embedding $latticecap'$ into a Boolean algebra using
Birkhoff's theorem, then checking the claim for all Boolean algebras.
It can also be checked directly (as suggested by Nghi Nguyen)
by showing that for $indexj=1,\dots,indexn$,
the meet of all joins of $indexj$-element subsets of $elementone,\dots,elementn$ is
invariant at each step.

On the other hand,
a lattice fails to be distributive if and only if
it contains five elements $baseseq,latticeb,latticec,0,1$ such that either the only relations
among them are implied by
\[
1 \geq baseseq,latticeb,latticec \geq 0
\]
(this lattice is sometimes called the \emph{diamond}),
or the only relations among them  are implied by
\[
1 \geq baseseq \geq latticeb \geq 0, \qquad 1 \geq latticec \geq 0
\]
(this lattice is sometimes called the \emph{pentagon}).
(For a proof, see the Birkhoff reference given above.) For each of these
examples, the initial sequence $baseseq,latticeb,latticec$ fails to determine the final
sequence; for the diamond, we can end up with $0, *, 1$ for
any of $* = baseseq,latticeb,latticec$, whereas for the pentagon we can end up with
$0, *, 1$ for any of $* = baseseq, latticeb$.

Consequently, the final sequence is determined by the initial sequence
if and only if $latticecap$ is distributive.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Start with a finite sequence $pineapple, blackcurrant, \dots, butternut$ of positive integers.
If possible, choose two indices $shoelaces < marshmallow$ such that $tangerine$ does not divide
$watermelon$, and replace $tangerine$ and $watermelon$ by $\mathrm{gcd}(tangerine, watermelon)$
and $\mathrm{lcm}(tangerine, watermelon)$, respectively. Prove that if this process is
repeated, it must eventually stop and the final sequence does not depend
on the choices made. (Note: gcd means greatest common divisor and lcm
means least common multiple.)
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
We first prove that the process stops. Note first that the product
$pineapple \cdots butternut$ remains constant, because
$tangerine \, watermelon = \gcd(tangerine, watermelon) \, \lcm(tangerine, watermelon)$. Moreover,
the last number in the sequence can never decrease, because it is always
replaced by its least common multiple with another number.
Since it is bounded above (by the product
of all of the numbers), the last number must eventually reach its
maximum value, after which it remains constant throughout. After this
happens, the next-to-last number will never decrease, so it eventually
becomes constant, and so on. After finitely many steps, all of the numbers
will achieve their final values, so
no more steps will be possible. This only happens when
$tangerine$ divides $watermelon$ for all pairs $shoelaces < marshmallow$.

We next check that there is only one possible final sequence.
For $gingerroot$ a prime and $caterpillar$ a nonnegative integer, we claim that the number
of integers in the list divisible by $gingerroot^{caterpillar}$ never changes. To see this,
suppose we replace $tangerine, watermelon$ by $\gcd(tangerine, watermelon),\lcm(tangerine,watermelon)$.
If neither of $tangerine, watermelon$ is divisible by $gingerroot^{caterpillar}$, then neither of
$\gcd(tangerine, watermelon),\lcm(tangerine,watermelon)$ is either.
If exactly one $tangerine, watermelon$ is divisible by $gingerroot^{caterpillar}$, then
$\lcm(tangerine,watermelon)$ is divisible by $gingerroot^{caterpillar}$ but $\gcd(tangerine, watermelon)$ is not.

$\gcd(tangerine, watermelon),\lcm(tangerine,watermelon)$ are as well.

If we started out with exactly $chandelier$ numbers not divisible by $gingerroot^{caterpillar}$,
then in the final sequence $a'_1, \dots, a'_{jellybeans}$, the numbers
$a'_{chandelier+1}, \dots, a'_{jellybeans}$ are divisible by $gingerroot^{caterpillar}$ while the numbers
$a'_1, \dots, a'_{chandelier}$ are not. Repeating this argument for each
pair $(gingerroot,caterpillar)$ such that $gingerroot^{caterpillar}$ divides the initial product
$pineapple,\dots,butternut$, we can determine the exact prime factorization
of each of $a'_1,\dots,a'_{jellybeans}$. This proves that the final sequence
is unique.

\textbf{Remark:}
(by David Savitt and Noam Elkies)
Here are two other ways to prove the termination.
One is to observe that $\prod_{shoelaces} tangerine^{\shoelaces}$
is \emph{strictly} increasing at each step, and bounded above by
$(pineapple\cdots butternut)^{jellybeans}$. The other is to notice that $pineapple$ is nonincreasing
but always positive, so eventually becomes constant; then
$blackcurrant$ is nonincreasing but always positive, and so on.

\textbf{Reinterpretation:}
For each $gingerroot$, consider the sequence consisting of the
exponents of $gingerroot$ in the prime factorizations of $pineapple,\dots,butternut$.
At each step, we pick two positions $i$ and $shoelaces$ such that the exponents
of some prime $gingerroot$ are in the wrong order at positions $i$ and $shoelaces$.
We then sort these two position into the correct order for every prime $gingerroot$
simultaneously.

It is clear that this can only terminate with all sequences being sorted
into the correct order. We must still check that the process terminates;
however, since all but finitely many of the exponent
sequences consist of all zeroes,
and each step makes a nontrivial switch in at least one of the other exponent
sequences, it is enough to check the case of a single exponent sequence.
This can be done as in the first solution.

\textbf{Remark:}
Abhinav Kumar suggests the following proof that the process always terminates
in at most  $\binom{jellybeans}{2}$ steps.
(This is a variant of the worst-case analysis of the \emph{bubble sort}
algorithm.)

Consider the number of pairs $(marshmallow,screwdriver)$
with $1 \leq marshmallow < screwdriver \leq jellybeans$ such that $watermelon$ does not divide $a_l$
(call these \emph{bad pairs}).
At each step, we find one bad pair $(i,shoelaces)$ and eliminate it, and we do not
touch any pairs that do not involve either $i$ or $shoelaces$.
If $i < marshmallow < shoelaces$, then neither of the pairs $(i,marshmallow)$ and $(marshmallow,shoelaces)$ can become
bad,
because $pomegranate$ is replaced by a divisor of itself, while $tangerine$ is replaced by
a multiple of itself. If $marshmallow < i$, then $(marshmallow,i)$ can only become a bad pair if
$pomegranate$ divided $pomegranate$ but not $tangerine$, in which case $(marshmallow,shoelaces)$ stops being bad.
Similarly, if $marshmallow > shoelaces$, then $(i,marshmallow)$ and $(shoelaces,marshmallow)$
either stay the same or switch status. Hence the number of bad pairs
goes down by at least 1 each time; since it is at most $\binom{jellybeans}{2}$
to begin with, this is an upper bound for the number of steps.

\textbf{Remark:}
This problem is closely related to the classification theorem for
finite abelian groups. Namely, if $pineapple,\dots,butternut$
and $a'_1,\dots,a'_{jellybeans}$ are the sequences obtained at two different
steps in the process, then the abelian groups
$\ZZ/pineapple \ZZ \times \cdots \times \ZZ/butternut \ZZ$
and
$\ZZ/a'_1 \ZZ \times \cdots \times \ZZ/a'_{jellybeans} \ZZ$
are isomorphic. The final sequence gives a canonical
presentation of this group; the terms of this sequence are called the
\emph{elementary divisors} or \emph{invariant factors} of the group.

\textbf{Remark:} (by Tom Belulovich)
A \emph{lattice} is a partially ordered set $hummingbird$ in which for
any two $paperclips,raincloud \in hummingbird$, there is a unique minimal element $fingernail$ with $fingernail \geq
paperclips$ and $fingernail \geq raincloud$, called the \emph{join} and denoted $paperclips \wedge raincloud$,
and there is a unique maximal element $fingernail$ with $fingernail \leq paperclips$ and $fingernail \leq raincloud$,
called the \emph{meet} and denoted $paperclips \vee raincloud$. In terms of a lattice $hummingbird$,
one can pose the following generalization of the given problem.
Start with $pineapple,\dots,butternut \in hummingbird$. If $i < shoelaces$ but $pomegranate \not\leq tangerine$,
it is permitted to replace $pomegranate, tangerine$ by $pomegranate \vee tangerine, pomegranate \wedge tangerine$,
respectively. The same argument as above shows that this always terminates
in at most $\binom{jellybeans}{2}$ steps. The question is, under what conditions on
the lattice $hummingbird$ is the final sequence uniquely determined by the initial
sequence?

It turns out that this holds if and only if $hummingbird$ is \emph{distributive},
i.e., for any $paperclips,raincloud,fingernail \in hummingbird$,
\[
paperclips \wedge (raincloud \vee fingernail)
= (paperclips \wedge raincloud) \vee (paperclips \wedge fingernail).
\]
(This is equivalent to the same axiom with the operations interchanged.)
For example, if $hummingbird$ is a \emph{Boolean algebra}, i.e., the set of subsets
of a given set $sailboats$ under inclusion, then $\wedge$ is union, $\vee$
is intersection, and the distributive law holds.
Conversely, any finite
distributive lattice is contained in a Boolean algebra by a theorem of
Birkhoff. The correspondence takes each  $fingernail \in hummingbird$ to the set of
$raincloud \in hummingbird$ such that $fingernail \geq raincloud$ and $raincloud$ cannot be written as a join
of two elements of $hummingbird \setminus \{raincloud\}$. (See for instance
Birkhoff, \textit{Lattice Theory}, Amer. Math. Soc., 1967.)

On one hand, if $hummingbird$ is distributive, it can be shown that the $shoelaces$-th term
of the final sequence is equal to the meet of $a_{i_1} \wedge \cdots
\wedge a_{i_{shoelaces}}$ over all sequences $1 \leq i_1 < \cdots < i_{shoelaces} \leq jellybeans$.
For instance, this can be checked by forming the smallest subset $hummingbird'$
of $hummingbird$ containing $pineapple,\dots,butternut$ and closed under meet and join,
then embedding $hummingbird'$ into a Boolean algebra using
Birkhoff's theorem, then checking the claim for all Boolean algebras.
It can also be checked directly (as suggested by Nghi Nguyen)
by showing that for $shoelaces=1,\dots,jellybeans$,
the meet of all joins of $shoelaces$-element subsets of $pineapple,\dots,butternut$ is
invariant at each step.

On the other hand,
a lattice fails to be distributive if and only if
it contains five elements $blueberries,horsewhip,toothbrush,0,1$ such that either the only relations
among them are implied by
\[
1 \geq blueberries,horsewhip,toothbrush \geq 0
\]
(this lattice is sometimes called the \emph{diamond}),
or the only relations among them  are implied by
\[
1 \geq blueberries \geq horsewhip \geq 0, \qquad 1 \geq toothbrush \geq 0
\]
(this lattice is sometimes called the \emph{pentagon}).
(For a proof, see the Birkhoff reference given above.) For each of these
examples, the initial sequence $blueberries,horsewhip,toothbrush$ fails to determine the final
sequence; for the diamond, we can end up with $0, *, 1$ for
any of $* = blueberries,horsewhip,toothbrush$, whereas for the pentagon we can end up with
$0, *, 1$ for any of $* = blueberries, horsewhip$.

Consequently, the final sequence is determined by the initial sequence
if and only if $hummingbird$ is distributive.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: Start with a finite sequence $negativeone, negativetwo, \dots, negativen$ of positive integers. If possible, choose two indices $endpoint < midpoint$ such that $negativejay$ does not divide $negativekay$, and replace $negativejay$ and $negativekay$ by $\mathrm{gcd}(negativejay, negativekay)$ and $\mathrm{lcm}(negativejay, negativekay)$, respectively. Prove that if this process is repeated, it must eventually stop and the final sequence does not depend on the choices made. (Note: gcd means greatest common divisor and lcm means least common multiple.)
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
We first prove that the process stops. Note first that the product $negativeone \cdots negativen$ remains constant, because $negativejay\,negativekay = \gcd(negativejay, negativekay)\,\lcm(negativejay, negativekay)$. Moreover, the last number in the sequence can never decrease, because it is always replaced by its least common multiple with another number. Since it is bounded above (by the product of all of the numbers), the last number must eventually reach its maximum value, after which it remains constant throughout. After this happens, the next-to-last number will never decrease, so it eventually becomes constant, and so on. After finitely many steps, all of the numbers will achieve their final values, so no more steps will be possible. This only happens when $negativejay$ divides $negativekay$ for all pairs $endpoint < midpoint$.

We next check that there is only one possible final sequence. For $composite$ a prime and $negativeexp$ a non-negative integer, we claim that the number of integers in the list divisible by $composite^{negativeexp}$ never changes. To see this, suppose we replace $negativejay, negativekay$ by $\gcd(negativejay, negativekay),\lcm(negativejay,negativekay)$. If neither of $negativejay, negativekay$ is divisible by $composite^{negativeexp}$, then neither of $\gcd(negativejay, negativekay),\lcm(negativejay,negativekay)$ is either. If exactly one of $negativejay, negativekay$ is divisible by $composite^{negativeexp}$, then $\lcm(negativejay,negativekay)$ is divisible by $composite^{negativeexp}$ but $\gcd(negativejay, negativekay)$ is not.

$\gcd(negativejay, negativekay),\lcm(negativejay,negativekay)$ are as well.

If we started out with exactly $emptiness$ numbers not divisible by $composite^{negativeexp}$, then in the final sequence $negative'_1, \dots, negative'_{emptysize}$, the numbers $negative'_{emptiness+1}, \dots, negative'_{emptysize}$ are divisible by $composite^{negativeexp}$ while the numbers $negative'_1, \dots, negative'_{emptiness}$ are not. Repeating this argument for each pair $(composite,negativeexp)$ such that $composite^{negativeexp}$ divides the initial product $negativeone,\dots,negativen$, we can determine the exact prime factorization of each of $negative'_1,\dots,negative'_{emptysize}$. This proves that the final sequence is unique.

\textbf{Remark:} (by David Savitt and Noam Elkies)
Here are two other ways to prove the termination. One is to observe that $\prod_{endpoint} negativejay^{endpoint}$ is \emph{strictly} increasing at each step, and bounded above by $(negativeone\cdots negativen)^{emptysize}$. The other is to notice that $negativeone$ is non-increasing but always positive, so eventually becomes constant; then $negativetwo$ is non-increasing but always positive, and so on.

\textbf{Reinterpretation:}
For each $composite$, consider the sequence consisting of the exponents of $composite$ in the prime factorizations of $negativeone,\dots,negativen$. At each step, we pick two positions $totality$ and $endpoint$ such that the exponents of some prime $composite$ are in the wrong order at positions $totality$ and $endpoint$. We then sort these two positions into the correct order for every prime $composite$ simultaneously.

It is clear that this can only terminate with all sequences being sorted into the correct order. We must still check that the process terminates; however, since all but finitely many of the exponent sequences consist of all zeroes, and each step makes a non-trivial switch in at least one of the other exponent sequences, it is enough to check the case of a single exponent sequence. This can be done as in the first solution.

\textbf{Remark:}
Abhinav Kumar suggests the following proof that the process always terminates in at most  $\binom{emptysize}{2}$ steps. (This is a variant of the worst-case analysis of the \emph{bubble sort} algorithm.)

Consider the number of pairs $(midpoint,terminal)$ with $1 \le midpoint < terminal \le emptysize$ such that $negativekay$ does not divide $negative_{terminal}$ (call these \emph{bad pairs}). At each step, we find one bad pair $(totality,endpoint)$ and eliminate it, and we do not touch any pairs that do not involve either $totality$ or $endpoint$. If $totality < midpoint < endpoint$, then neither of the pairs $(totality,midpoint)$ and $(midpoint,endpoint)$ can become bad, because $negativeindex$ is replaced by a divisor of itself, while $negativejay$ is replaced by a multiple of itself. If $midpoint < totality$, then $(midpoint,totality)$ can only become a bad pair if $negative_{midpoint}$ divided $negativeindex$ but not $negativejay$, in which case $(midpoint,endpoint)$ stops being bad. Similarly, if $midpoint > endpoint$, then $(totality,midpoint)$ and $(endpoint,midpoint)$ either stay the same or switch status. Hence the number of bad pairs goes down by at least 1 each time; since it is at most $\binom{emptysize}{2}$ to begin with, this is an upper bound for the number of steps.

\textbf{Remark:}
This problem is closely related to the classification theorem for finite abelian groups. Namely, if $negativeone,\dots,negativen$ and $negative'_1,\dots,negative'_{emptysize}$ are the sequences obtained at two different steps in the process, then the abelian groups $\mathbb{Z}/negativeone\,\mathbb{Z} \times \cdots \times \mathbb{Z}/negativen\,\mathbb{Z}$ and $\mathbb{Z}/negative'_1\,\mathbb{Z} \times \cdots \times \mathbb{Z}/negative'_{emptysize}\,\mathbb{Z}$ are isomorphic. The final sequence gives a canonical presentation of this group; the terms of this sequence are called the \emph{elementary divisors} or \emph{invariant factors} of the group.

\textbf{Remark:} (by Tom Belulovich)
A \emph{lattice} is a partially ordered set $nonlattice$ in which for any two $constantval,fixedpoint \in nonlattice$, there is a unique minimal element $steadystate$ with $steadystate \ge constantval$ and $steadystate \ge fixedpoint$, called the \emph{join} and denoted $constantval \wedge fixedpoint$, and there is a unique maximal element $steadystate$ with $steadystate \le constantval$ and $steadystate \le fixedpoint$, called the \emph{meet} and denoted $constantval \vee fixedpoint$. In terms of a lattice $nonlattice$, one can pose the following generalization of the given problem. Start with $negativeone,\dots,negativen \in nonlattice$. If $totality < endpoint$ but $negativeindex \not\le negativejay$, it is permitted to replace $negativeindex, negativejay$ by $negativeindex \vee negativejay, negativeindex \wedge negativejay$, respectively. The same argument as above shows that this always terminates in at most $\binom{emptysize}{2}$ steps. The question is, under what conditions on the lattice $nonlattice$ is the final sequence uniquely determined by the initial sequence?

It turns out that this holds if and only if $nonlattice$ is \emph{distributive}, i.e., for any $constantval,fixedpoint,steadystate \in nonlattice$,
\[
constantval \wedge (fixedpoint \vee steadystate)
= (constantval \wedge fixedpoint) \vee (constantval \wedge steadystate).
\]
(This is equivalent to the same axiom with the operations interchanged.) For example, if $nonlattice$ is a \emph{Boolean algebra}, i.e., the set of subsets of a given set $unordered$ under inclusion, then $\wedge$ is union, $\vee$ is intersection, and the distributive law holds. Conversely, any finite distributive lattice is contained in a Boolean algebra by a theorem of Birkhoff. The correspondence takes each  $constantval \in nonlattice$ to the set of $fixedpoint \in nonlattice$ such that $constantval \ge fixedpoint$ and $fixedpoint$ cannot be written as a join of two elements of $nonlattice \setminus \{fixedpoint\}$. (See for instance Birkhoff, \textit{Lattice Theory}, Amer. Math. Soc., 1967.)

On one hand, if $nonlattice$ is distributive, it can be shown that the $endpoint$-th term of the final sequence is equal to the meet of $negative_{totality_1} \wedge \cdots \wedge negative_{totality_{endpoint}}$ over all sequences $1 \le totality_1 < \cdots < totality_{endpoint} \le emptysize$. For instance, this can be checked by forming the smallest subset $nonlattice'$ of $nonlattice$ containing $negativeone,\dots,negativen$ and closed under meet and join, then embedding $nonlattice'$ into a Boolean algebra using Birkhoff's theorem, then checking the claim for all Boolean algebras. It can also be checked directly (as suggested by Nghi Nguyen) by showing that for $endpoint=1,\dots,emptysize$, the meet of all joins of $endpoint$-element subsets of $negativeone,\dots,negativen$ is invariant at each step.

On the other hand, a lattice fails to be distributive if and only if it contains five elements $negative,emptyset,completeset,0,1$ such that either the only relations among them are implied by
\[
1 \ge negative,emptyset,completeset \ge 0
\]
(this lattice is sometimes called the \emph{diamond}), or the only relations among them  are implied by
\[
1 \ge negative \ge emptyset \ge 0, \qquad 1 \ge completeset \ge 0
\]
(this lattice is sometimes called the \emph{pentagon}). (For a proof, see the Birkhoff reference given above.) For each of these examples, the initial sequence $negative,emptyset,completeset$ fails to determine the final sequence; for the diamond, we can end up with $0, * , 1$ for any of $* = negative,emptyset,completeset$, whereas for the pentagon we can end up with $0, * , 1$ for any of $* = negative, emptyset$.

Consequently, the final sequence is determined by the initial sequence if and only if $nonlattice$ is distributive.

garbled_string.question:
Start with a finite sequence $blrfkawu, hjsmcine, \dots, vgplawqe$ of positive integers.
If possible, choose two indices $wlbqstne < ufrezhia$ such that $nvyztrso$ does not divide
$kmfsudjq$, and replace $nvyztrso$ and $kmfsudjq$ by $\mathrm{gcd}(nvyztrso, kmfsudjq)$
and $\mathrm{lcm}(nvyztrso, kmfsudjq)$, respectively. Prove that if this process is
repeated, it must eventually stop and the final sequence does not depend
on the choices made. (Note: gcd means greatest common divisor and lcm
means least common multiple.)
garbled_string.solution:
We first prove that the process stops. Note first that the product
$blrfkawu \cdots vgplawqe$ remains constant, because
$nvyztrso\,kmfsudjq = \gcd(nvyztrso,kmfsudjq)\,\lcm(nvyztrso,kmfsudjq)$. Moreover,
the last number in the sequence can never decrease, because it is always
replaced by its least common multiple with another number.
Since it is bounded above (by the product of all of the numbers), the last
number must eventually reach its maximum value, after which it remains
constant throughout. After this happens, the next-to-last number will never
decrease, so it eventually becomes constant, and so on. After finitely many
steps, all of the numbers will achieve their final values, so no more steps
will be possible. This only happens when $nvyztrso$ divides $kmfsudjq$ for
all pairs $wlbqstne < ufrezhia$.

We next check that there is only one possible final sequence.
For $duhczeto$ a prime and $kzputhme$ a non-negative integer, we claim that
the number of integers in the list divisible by $duhczeto^{kzputhme}$ never
changes. To see this, suppose we replace $nvyztrso, kmfsudjq$ by
$\gcd(nvyztrso,kmfsudjq),\lcm(nvyztrso,kmfsudjq)$. If neither of
$nvyztrso, kmfsudjq$ is divisible by $duhczeto^{kzputhme}$, then neither of
$\gcd(nvyztrso,kmfsudjq),\lcm(nvyztrso,kmfsudjq)$ is either. If exactly one
of $nvyztrso, kmfsudjq$ is divisible by $duhczeto^{kzputhme}$, then
$\lcm(nvyztrso,kmfsudjq)$ is divisible by $duhczeto^{kzputhme}$ but
$\gcd(nvyztrso,kmfsudjq)$ is not; $\gcd(nvyztrso,kmfsudjq),\lcm(nvyztrso,kmfsudjq)$ are as well.

If we started out with exactly $zlefnsut$ numbers not divisible by
$duhczeto^{kzputhme}$, then in the final sequence $blrfkawu', \dots,
vgplawqe'$, the numbers $a'_{zlefnsut+1}, \dots, vgplawqe'$ are divisible by
$duhczeto^{kzputhme}$ while the numbers $blrfkawu', \dots, a'_{zlefnsut}$
are not. Repeating this argument for each pair $(duhczeto,kzputhme)$ such
that $duhczeto^{kzputhme}$ divides the initial product
$blrfkawu,\dots,vgplawqe$, we can determine the exact prime factorization of
each of $blrfkawu',\dots,vgplawqe'$. This proves that the final sequence is
unique.

\textbf{Remark:} (by David Savitt and Noam Elkies)
Here are two other ways to prove the termination.
One is to observe that $\prod_{wlbqstne} nvyztrso^{wlbqstne}$ is \emph{strictly}
increasing at each step, and bounded above by $(blrfkawu\cdots vgplawqe)^{sbliavwm}$.
The other is to notice that $blrfkawu$ is non-increasing but always positive,
so eventually becomes constant; then $hjsmcine$ is non-increasing but always
positive, and so on.

\textbf{Reinterpretation:}
For each $duhczeto$, consider the sequence consisting of the exponents of
$duhczeto$ in the prime factorizations of $blrfkawu,\dots,vgplawqe$. At each
step, we pick two positions $pghdrkvo$ and $wlbqstne$ such that the
exponents of some prime $duhczeto$ are in the wrong order at those
positions. We then sort these two positions into the correct order for
every prime $duhczeto$ simultaneously.

It is clear that this can only terminate with all sequences being sorted
into the correct order. We must still check that the process terminates;
however, since all but finitely many of the exponent sequences consist of
all zeroes, and each step makes a non-trivial switch in at least one of the
other exponent sequences, it is enough to check the case of a single
exponent sequence. This can be done as in the first solution.

\textbf{Remark:} Abhinav Kumar suggests the following proof that the process
always terminates in at most  $\binom{sbliavwm}{2}$ steps. (This is a variant
of the worst-case analysis of the \emph{bubble sort} algorithm.)

Consider the number of pairs $(ufrezhia,oymsdckw)$ with $1 \le ufrezhia <
oymsdckw \le sbliavwm$ such that $a_{ufrezhia}$ does not divide
$a_{oymsdckw}$ (call these \emph{bad pairs}). At each step, we find one bad
pair $(pghdrkvo,wlbqstne)$ and eliminate it, and we do not touch any pairs
that do not involve either $pghdrkvo$ or $wlbqstne$. If $pghdrkvo < ufrezhia <
wlbqstne$, then neither of the pairs $(pghdrkvo,ufrezhia)$ and
$(ufrezhia,wlbqstne)$ can become bad, because $a_{pghdrkvo}$ is replaced by a
divisor of itself, while $a_{wlbqstne}$ is replaced by a multiple of itself.
If $ufrezhia < pghdrkvo$, then $(ufrezhia,pghdrkvo)$ can only become a bad pair if
$a_{ufrezhia}$ divided $a_{pghdrkvo}$ but not $a_{wlbqstne}$, in which case
$(ufrezhia,wlbqstne)$ stops being bad. Similarly, if $ufrezhia > wlbqstne$, then
$(pghdrkvo,ufrezhia)$ and $(wlbqstne,ufrezhia)$ either stay the same or switch
status. Hence the number of bad pairs goes down by at least 1 each time;
since it is at most $\binom{sbliavwm}{2}$ to begin with, this is an upper
bound for the number of steps.

\textbf{Remark:}
This problem is closely related to the classification theorem for finite
abelian groups. Namely, if $blrfkawu,\dots,vgplawqe$ and
$blrfkawu',\dots,vgplawqe'$ are the sequences obtained at two different
steps in the process, then the abelian groups
$\ZZ/blrfkawu\,\ZZ \times \cdots \times \ZZ/vgplawqe\,\ZZ$ and
$\ZZ/blrfkawu'\,\ZZ \times \cdots \times \ZZ/vgplawqe'\,\ZZ$ are isomorphic.
The final sequence gives a canonical presentation of this group; the terms
of this sequence are called the \emph{elementary divisors} or
\emph{invariant factors} of the group.

\textbf{Remark:} (by Tom Belulovich)
A \emph{lattice} is a partially ordered set $fidwusoy$ in which for any two
$sjbravlm,entufgzc \in fidwusoy$, there is a unique minimal element
$chixaryp$ with $chixaryp \ge sjbravlm$ and $chixaryp \ge entufgzc$, called the
\emph{join} and denoted $sjbravlm \wedge entufgzc$, and there is a unique
maximal element $chixaryp$ with $chixaryp \le sjbravlm$ and
$chixaryp \le entufgzc$, called the \emph{meet} and denoted
$sjbravlm \vee entufgzc$. In terms of a lattice $fidwusoy$, one can pose the
following generalization of the given problem. Start with
$qzxwvtnp_1,\dots,qzxwvtnp_{sbliavwm} \in fidwusoy$. If $pghdrkvo < wlbqstne$ but
$qzxwvtnp_{pghdrkvo} \not\le qzxwvtnp_{wlbqstne}$, it is permitted to replace
$qzxwvtnp_{pghdrkvo}, qzxwvtnp_{wlbqstne}$ by $qzxwvtnp_{pghdrkvo} \vee
qzxwvtnp_{wlbqstne}, qzxwvtnp_{pghdrkvo} \wedge qzxwvtnp_{wlbqstne}$,
respectively. The same argument as above shows that this always terminates
in at most $\binom{sbliavwm}{2}$ steps. The question is, under what
conditions on the lattice $fidwusoy$ is the final sequence uniquely
determined by the initial sequence?

It turns out that this holds if and only if $fidwusoy$ is
\emph{distributive}, i.e., for any $sjbravlm,entufgzc,chixaryp \in fidwusoy$,
\[
sjbravlm \wedge (entufgzc \vee chixaryp)
= (sjbravlm \wedge entufgzc) \vee (sjbravlm \wedge chixaryp).
\]
(This is equivalent to the same axiom with the operations interchanged.)
For example, if $fidwusoy$ is a \emph{Boolean algebra}, i.e., the set of
subsets of a given set $lagpwenr$ under inclusion, then $\wedge$ is union,
$\vee$ is intersection, and the distributive law holds. Conversely, any
finite distributive lattice is contained in a Boolean algebra by a theorem
of Birkhoff. The correspondence takes each  $sjbravlm \in fidwusoy$ to the
set of $entufgzc \in fidwusoy$ such that $sjbravlm \ge entufgzc$ and
$entufgzc$ cannot be written as a join of two elements of
$fidwusoy \setminus \{entufgzc\}$. (See for instance Birkhoff,
\textit{Lattice Theory}, Amer. Math. Soc., 1967.)

On one hand, if $fidwusoy$ is distributive, it can be shown that the
$wlbqstne$-th term of the final sequence is equal to the meet of
$qzxwvtnp_{pghdrkvo_1} \wedge \cdots \wedge
qzxwvtnp_{pghdrkvo_{wlbqstne}}$ over all sequences
$1 \le pghdrkvo_1 < \cdots < pghdrkvo_{wlbqstne} \le sbliavwm$. For
instance, this can be checked by forming the smallest subset $fidwusoy'$ of
$fidwusoy$ containing $qzxwvtnp_1,\dots,qzxwvtnp_{sbliavwm}$ and closed under
meet and join, then embedding $fidwusoy'$ into a Boolean algebra using
Birkhoff's theorem, then checking the claim for all Boolean algebras. It can
also be checked directly (as suggested by Nghi Nguyen) by showing that for
$wlbqstne=1,\dots,sbliavwm$, the meet of all joins of $wlbqstne$-element
subsets of $qzxwvtnp_1,\dots,qzxwvtnp_{sbliavwm}$ is invariant at each step.

On the other hand, a lattice fails to be distributive if and only if it
contains five elements $qzxwvtnp,tqefyrlg,rxpjamod,0,1$ such that either the
only relations among them are implied by $1 \ge qzxwvtnp,tqefyrlg,rxpjamod
\ge 0$ (this lattice is sometimes called the \emph{diamond}), or the only
relations among them  are implied by $1 \ge qzxwvtnp \ge tqefyrlg \ge 0,
\qquad 1 \ge rxpjamod \ge 0$ (this lattice is sometimes called the
\emph{pentagon}). (For a proof, see the Birkhoff reference given above.) For
each of these examples, the initial sequence $qzxwvtnp,tqefyrlg,rxpjamod$
fails to determine the final sequence; for the diamond, we can end up with
$0, *, 1$ for any of $* = qzxwvtnp,tqefyrlg,rxpjamod$, whereas for the pentagon we can
end up with $0, *, 1$ for any of $* = qzxwvtnp, tqefyrlg$.

Consequently, the final sequence is determined by the initial sequence if
and only if $fidwusoy$ is distributive.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $n\ge 2$ and let $b_1,b_2,\dots ,b_n$ be positive integers written in a row.
Whenever there is an index $t$ with $1\le t<n$ for which $\,b_t\nmid b_{t+1}$, you are allowed to
replace the 
_adjacent_ pair $(b_t,b_{t+1})$ by
\[
\bigl(\gcd(b_t,b_{t+1}),\;\operatorname{lcm}(b_t,b_{t+1})\bigr)\qquad\text{(in this order).}
\]
Prove that
(a) this procedure must stop after finitely many steps, and
(b) the final sequence is the same no matter which legal adjacent pair is
chosen at each move.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout the proof write x\lor y= lcm(x,y) and x\land y= gcd(x,y).  A move replaces an adjacent pair (x,y) by (x\land y,x\lor y) precisely when x\nmid y.

Step 1.  A strictly increasing bounded potential.
------------------------------------------------
Assign weights 2^{i-1} to position i and define

    \Phi (b_1,\ldots ,b_n)=\prod _{i=1}^n b_i^{2^{i-1}}.

Under a move at positions t,t+1 set x=b_t, y=b_{t+1}, write x=g d_1, y=g d_2 with g=gcd(x,y), gcd(d_1,d_2)=1, and x\nmid y\Rightarrow d_1>1.  Then x\land y=g, x\lor y=g d_1d_2, and

    \Phi _{new}/\Phi _{old}
      = g^{2^{t-1}}\cdot (g d_1d_2)^{2^t}  \
      /( (g d_1)^{2^{t-1}}\cdot (g d_2)^{2^t} )
      = d_1^{2^t-2^{t-1}} = d_1^{2^{t-1}} > 1.

Since \prod b_i remains constant (because gcd(x,y)\cdot lcm(x,y)=x\cdot y) and each exponent 2^{i-1}\leq 2^{n-1}, we have

    \Phi =\prod  b_i^{2^{i-1}} \leq  \prod  b_i^{2^{n-1}} = (\prod  b_i)^{2^{n-1}},

so \Phi  is an increasing positive integer bounded above, hence the process terminates.

Step 2.  Characterisation of the stopping configuration.
-------------------------------------------------------
No further moves are possible exactly when no adjacent pair is out of divisibility order, i.e.

    b_1\mid b_2\mid \ldots \mid b_n.

Step 3.  Prime-power count invariants.
---------------------------------------
For each prime p and m\geq 0, let

    \sigma _{p,m}(b_1,\ldots ,b_n) = |{i : p^m divides b_i}|.

Replacing (x,y) by (gcd(x,y),lcm(x,y)) preserves the number of terms divisible by p^m (check cases p^m\nmid x,y; p^m|x,y; exactly one of x,y divisible), so each \sigma _{p,m} is invariant.

Step 4.  Uniqueness of the final sequence.
-----------------------------------------
Let (c_1,\ldots ,c_n) be any sorted-divisibility sequence (c_1\mid \ldots \mid c_n) with the same invariants \sigma _{p,m}.  For each prime p, the exponents v_p(c_1)\leq \ldots \leq v_p(c_n) form a nondecreasing sequence, and the requirement that precisely \sigma _{p,m} of the c_i be \geq  m forces these exponents uniquely.  Since this holds for every p, (c_1,\ldots ,c_n) is uniquely determined.

Combining termination (Step 1) with uniqueness of the endpoint (Steps 2-4) completes the proof that the process stops in finitely many moves and yields the same final list regardless of choices.

core_steps: Define a bounded, strictly increasing potential function (e.g. Π a_i^i) to force termination.
Observe that the process halts exactly when the sequence is divisibility-sorted: a_1 | a_2 | … | a_n.
Prove that, for every prime power p^m, the number of terms divisible by p^m is invariant under each move.
Use all these per-prime-power counts to reconstruct, hence to show uniqueness of, the final sorted sequence.

slot1.description: Index whose monotonic behaviour is tracked in the simpler termination proof (could be first, last, kth, etc.).
slot1.original: the last entry a_n is non-decreasing

slot2.description: The exponents chosen in the potential function; any strictly increasing positive sequence works.

slot3.description: The explicit upper-bound constant used for the potential function.

slot4.description: The ordering condition on the chosen pair; only an ordering that makes the replacement rule deterministic is needed.
slot4.original: require j < k before replacing a_j,a_k

slot5.description: Universe of prime powers tracked for the invariance argument; any collection containing all prime powers dividing the initial product suffices.
slot5.original: all (p,m) with p prime, m ≥ 0 such that p^m | a_1⋯a_n
